expected_baseline_failures: cross-chapter motif repetition observable in operator side-by-side
endpoint-landing weak on at least one chapter
fixture_metadata.scene_contract_population_target: approx 4 of 9 fields per scene; remainder null is part of the test
fixture_metadata.derived_from: structural twin of novel-1778411555121 (2026-05-10 evidence run); central conceit altered from wound-transfer to debt-transfer to avoid identity with fantasy-healer
fixture_metadata.notes: Adjusted-B2 P1. The conceit is a pressure system whose every deployment invites further dramatization (debt-by-debt re-dramatization is structurally analogous to wound-by-wound). The casting gap is deliberate so the writer is forced to coin officer/clerk/witness names, reproducing the halluc-ungrounded retry profile observed in chapter 1 and chapter 2 of the live evidence run.

concept.premise: A debt-binder discovers she can move financial ruin between people — not absolve it, transfer it. The crown wants her to move sovereign default onto the families of dead soldiers. A dying chancellor offers her a choice: move his ruin onto a debtor's prison foreman and save the treasury, or refuse and watch the city's grain supply collapse.

characters.name: Maren Ailish
characters.description: Crown debt-binder, mid-20s. Found her ability four months ago when a creditor's curse refused to settle on the named debtor and slid sideways into the witness. Counting-house trained, mathematically blunt, hates the war-loans but believes the families are owed honest accounting. Every transfer leaves a phantom interest accruing in her own credit — small, steady, not yet ruinous.

characters.name: Sergeant Tovin
characters.description: Treasury escort assigned to Maren after the riots. Twenty-one, lied about his birth-village to enlist into the city watch. Quiet, watchful, has seen too much foreclosure. The only person who treats Maren like a person instead of an instrument. Lost his cousin to debtor's prison the previous winter.

characters.name: Chancellor Halric
characters.description: The dying chancellor. Sixty-two, legendary auditor, built the war-loan program on personal guarantees. Not corrupt — genuinely believes the loans will save more families than they ruin. His ruin is a slow ledger collapse, certain by spring. He has had seven days to think about the math of one foreman's life versus a hundred thousand stomachs.
